blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلْعَصْرِ (١)
By Time! (1)
إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَفِى خُسْرٍ (٢)
Man is indeed in loss, (2)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ (٣)
except those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and enjoin one another to [follow] the truth, and enjoin one another to patience. (3)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Asr"

It is well-known that this Surah was revealed in "Makkah," although some have suggested the possibility of it being "Madani." The short rhythm of the verses and its tone also testify to its Makkan origin.

In any case, the comprehensiveness of this Surah is such that, according to some commentators, all the sciences and objectives of the Quran are summarized within it. In other words, despite its brevity, this Surah organizes and presents a comprehensive and complete program for human happiness.

It begins first with a meaningful oath by "Asr," the interpretation of which you will soon see. Then, it speaks of the loss inherent in the lives of all human beings, which is rooted in the nature of gradual life. Afterward, it exempts only one group from this general principle: those who possess the following four-point program:

Faith, righteous deeds, exhorting one another to truth, and exhorting one another to patience. These four principles, in reality, encompass the ideological, practical, individual, and social programs of Islam.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

من قرأ "وَ الْعَصْرِ" فى نوافله بعثه اللَّه يوم القيامة مشرقا وجهه ضاحكا سنه قريرة عينه حتى يدخل الجنة:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Al-Asr' in his supererogatory prayers (Nawafil), Allah will raise him on the Day of Resurrection with a radiant face, a smiling countenance, and eyes delighted (by Divine blessings), until he enters Paradise!" [1] It is evident that all this honor, joy, and happiness belong to the one who implements these four principles in his life, not merely one who contents himself with recitation.

[1]: "Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 545.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ وَ الْعَصْرِ (1) إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ (2) إِلاَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَ تَواصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَ تَواصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ (3)

Commentary

The Only Way to Salvation

At the beginning of this Surah, we encounter a new oath; He says: "By the Al-Asr (Time)!" (وَ الْعَصْرِ).

The word "Asr" originally means "to squeeze" or "to press," and was later applied to the time of afternoon because daily programs and activities are wrapped up and compressed at that time.

Subsequently, this word has been used to mean absolute "time," the eras of human history, or a specific part of time, such as the era of the advent of Islam and the uprising of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the like. Therefore, commentators have mentioned many possibilities in interpreting this oath:

  1. Some consider it a reference to the "afternoon" time itself, by the clue that in other verses of the Quran, oaths are taken by the beginning of the day, such as "By the morning brightness" (Surah Ad-Duha, Verse 1) or "And by the dawn when it brightens" (Al-Muddaththir, 34).

This oath is due to the importance of this time of day, as it is the time of change in the system of life and human activity. Daily tasks come to an end, birds and animals return to their nests, the sun sinks into the horizon, and the air gradually turns toward darkness.

This transformation directs man toward the eternal power of Allah that governs this system, and in truth, it is one of the signs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and a sign among the signs of the Lord that is worthy of an oath.

  1. Others have considered it a reference to the entirety of time and human history, which is full of lessons, shocking events, and awakening incidents. For this reason, it possesses such greatness that it is worthy of a Divine oath.

  2. Some have pointed to a specific part of this time, such as the era of the uprising of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the era of the uprising of Imam al-Mahdi عليه السلام, which has had and continues to have a unique distinction and greatness in human history, and they consider the oath to refer to that. [1]

  3. Some have returned to the linguistic root of the word, considering the oath to refer to the types of pressures and difficulties that occur throughout human life, which wake them from the sleep of negligence, remind them of the Great God, and nurture the spirit of steadfastness.

  4. Others consider it a reference to the "Perfect Human Beings" (al-Insan al-Kamil) who are the essence (Asarah) of the realm of existence and the world of creation.

  5. Finally, some have regarded it as referring to the "Asr Prayer," because of the special importance it holds among the prayers; for they consider the "Middle Prayer" (al-Salat al-Wusta), which the Quran emphasizes specifically, to be the Asr prayer.

While these interpretations do not contradict one another and all could be gathered within the meaning of the verse—with the oath being taken by all these important matters—the most appropriate among them is "Asr" in the sense of time and human history. This is because, as we have often stated, the oaths of the Quran are always proportionate to the subject for which the oath is taken. It is certain that the loss of human beings in life is the result of the passing of the time of their lives, or the era of the uprising of the Final Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله because the four-point program at the end of the Surah was revealed in such an era.

From what has been said, the greatness of the Quranic verses and the breadth of its concepts become clearly evident—how a single word can be so meaningful and worthy of deep and diverse interpretations.

[1]: In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read regarding the interpretation of the verse "By the Asr, indeed man is in loss," he said: "The Asr is the era of the reappearance of al-Qa'im (the Uprising One)." (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 666, Hadith 5).

In the next verse, He refers to the matter for which this important oath was taken, saying: "Most certainly, all human beings are in loss" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ).

They lose the capital of their existence whether they want to or not; the hours, days, months, and years of life pass quickly, spiritual and material strengths decline, and power and ability diminish.

Yes, man is like someone who possesses a vast capital, and without his choice, a portion of that capital is taken from him every day. This is the nature of worldly life—the nature of continuous loss! A heart has a specific capacity for beating; when that capacity and power end, the heart stops on its own, even without a defect, cause, or illness being involved. This is the case if it does not stop earlier due to illness. The same applies to other systems of human existence and the capitals of his various talents.

"Khusr" and "Khusran," as Raghib says in "Al-Mufradat," mean the reduction of capital. Sometimes it is attributed to the person, and it is said "so-and-so suffered a loss," and sometimes it is attributed to the action itself, and it is said "his trade suffered a loss." This word is mostly used for external capitals like wealth and status, and sometimes for internal capitals like health, sanity, faith, and reward. This is exactly what Allah the Almighty has referred to as "Manifest Loss" (Khusran Mubin) where He says: "Indeed, the losers are those who have lost themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection. Unquestionably, that is the manifest loss" (Az-Zumar, 15). [1]

Fakhr al-Razi, in the commentary of this verse, quotes a statement whose summary is as follows: "One of the elders of the past said: I learned the meaning of this Surah from an ice-seller. He was shouting and saying: 'Have mercy on the one whose capital is melting! Have mercy on the one whose capital is melting!' I said to myself: This is the meaning of 'Indeed, man is in loss.' Time passes over him and his life ends, and if he does not earn a reward, he is in a state of loss." [2]

In any case, from the perspective of the Islamic worldview, the world is a marketplace of trade, just as we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Hadi عليه السلام: "The world is a market; some people profited in it and others lost." [3] The verse under discussion says everyone in this great market is losing, except for one group whose program is explained in the next verse.

Yes, there is only one way to prevent this great loss and inevitable, forced deficit—only one way, which is mentioned in the final verse of this Surah.

[1]: "Mufradat al-Raghib," root "Kh-S-R." [2]: "Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi," Vol. 32, p. 85. [3]: "Tuhaf al-Uqul," p. 361 (Words of Imam al-Hadi عليه السلام).

He says: "Except those who believe and do righteous deeds, and exhort one another to truth, and exhort one another to patience" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَ تَواصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَ تَواصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ).

In other words, the thing that can prevent this great loss and turn it into a massive profit and benefit is that, in exchange for losing this capital, one acquires a more precious and valuable capital that not only fills the void of the lost capital but is tens, hundreds, and thousands of times greater and better than it.

Every breath a person takes brings him one step closer to death, as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said in that luminous phrase: "The breath of a man is his step toward his death." [1] Therefore, every beat of a person's heart brings him one step closer to the end of his life, and thus, in the face of this certain loss, something must be done to fill the void.

A group loses the precious capitals of life and existence and, in exchange, acquires a small or large amount of wealth, a humble house, or a beautiful palace. A group loses all this capital to reach a position of status. And groups lose it in the path of indulgence and fleeting material pleasures. Certainly, none of these can be the price of that great capital; its price is only and only the pleasure of Allah and the station of proximity to Him.

Or as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said: "Indeed, there is no price for your souls except Paradise, so do not sell them for anything less." [1] Or as it appears in the supplication of the month of Rajab from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Disappointed are those who arrive at other than You, and in loss are those who seek other than You!" And it is not without reason that one of the names of the Day of Resurrection is "The Day of Mutual Loss" (Yawm al-Taghabun), as stated in Verse 9 of Surah At-Taghabun: "That is the Day of Mutual Loss"—the day it becomes clear who has been cheated and has suffered loss.

The beauty of the matter and the grace of the issue is that, on one hand, the buyer of the capitals of human existence is the Great God: "Indeed, Allah has purchased from the believers..." (At-Tawbah, 111). On the other hand, He also purchases small capitals: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it" (Az-Zalzalah, 7). And on the third hand, He pays a great price for it—sometimes tenfold, sometimes up to seven hundred times, and sometimes more: "In every ear a hundred grains; and Allah multiplies for whom He wills" (Al-Baqarah, 261). And as it has come in the supplications: "O He who accepts the little and pardons the much." And on the fourth hand, even though He Himself provided all the capitals, He is so Generous that He returns and buys those very same things at the highest price!

[1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, No. 74. [1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, No. 456.

Key Points The Four-Point Program for Happiness

It is noteworthy that the Quran has organized a comprehensive program to save one from that great loss, which relies on four principles:

The first principle in this program is the issue of "Faith," which forms the foundation of all human activities. This is because a person's practical efforts stem from his intellectual and belief-based foundations, unlike animals whose movements are due to instinctive motives. In other words, human actions are a manifestation of his beliefs and thoughts. For this reason, all Divine Prophets, before anything else, dealt with reforming the ideological foundations of nations, specifically fighting against polytheism (Shirk), which is the source of all kinds of vices, miseries, and divisions. Interestingly, "Faith" is mentioned here in an absolute sense to include faith in all sacred things—ranging from faith in Allah and His attributes to faith in the Resurrection, the Reckoning, the Reward, the Heavenly Books, the Divine Prophets, and their successors.

In the second principle, it addresses the fruit of the fertile and productive tree of faith, speaking of "Righteous Deeds." What a broad and content-rich expression! Yes, "Salihat" are "worthy deeds"—not just acts of worship, not only spending in the way of Allah, not only Jihad in the way of God, not only the acquisition of knowledge, but every worthy deed that becomes a means for the perfection of souls, the nurturing of ethics, proximity to Allah, and the progress of human society in all fields. This expression even includes small acts like removing a bothersome stone from the people's path, up to saving millions upon millions of humans from misguidance and the spreading of the religion of truth and justice throughout the world. And if in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام "righteous deeds" are interpreted as "consolation and equality with religious brothers," it is by way of mentioning a clear example. Sometimes righteous deeds may be performed by some non-believers, but they are certainly not deep-rooted, stable, or widespread, because they do not stem from deep Divine motives and lack comprehensiveness. The Quran here specifically brings "Salihat" in the "plural" form, a plural accompanied by "Alif and Lam" (the definite article), which carries a general meaning. It expresses the reality that the way to prevent that natural and inevitable loss, after faith, is to perform all righteous deeds, not just contenting oneself with one or a few righteous acts. Truly, if faith takes root deeply in a person's soul, it manifests such effects. Faith is not merely a thought or a belief in the corners of the soul devoid of any impact; faith colors the entire existence of a person. Faith is like a bright lamp lit inside a room; it not only illuminates the space of the room but its rays fall outside through all the windows of the room. Anyone passing by outside understands well that a bright lamp is lit there. Similarly, when the lamp of faith is lit in the house of a person's heart, its light is reflected from the "tongue," "eye," "ear," and the "hands and feet" of the person. The movements of each of them show that there is a light in the heart whose rays have shone outward. For this reason, in the verses of the Quran, "righteous deeds" are often mentioned alongside "faith" as "necessary and inseparable" from one another. In Verse 97 of Surah An-Nahl, we read: "Whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer—We will surely cause him to live a good life." And in Verse 100 of Surah Al-Mu'minun, we read that the regret of the evildoers after departing from this world is why they did not perform righteous deeds; hence, with great insistence, they request to return to perform righteous deeds: "My Lord, send me back that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind." And in Verse 51 of Surah Al-Mu'minun, it is mentioned that Allah commands His messengers: "O messengers, eat from the good foods and work righteousness."

Since faith and righteous deeds can never endure unless there is a movement in society to call toward the truth and its recognition on one hand, and a call to steadfastness and patience in the path of fulfilling this call on the other, following these two principles, He points to two other principles which are, in fact, the guarantee for the implementation of the two fundamental principles of "faith" and "righteous deeds."

In the third principle, He refers to the issue of "Exhorting one another to truth," meaning a collective and public call toward the truth, so that everyone may distinguish truth from falsehood well, never forget it, and never deviate from it in the path of life. "Tawasaw" from the root "Tawasi," as Raghib has brought in Al-Mufradat, means that some exhort others. "Haqq" (Truth) means "reality" or "correspondence with reality." In the book "Wujuh al-Quran," twelve meanings and instances of usage for this word in the Holy Quran are mentioned, such as God, the Quran, Islam, Monotheism, Justice, Truthfulness, being manifest, being obligatory, and the like; but all of them return to the root mentioned above. In any case, the phrase "exhort one another to truth" has a very broad meaning that includes "enjoining the good and forbidding the evil," as well as "teaching and guiding the ignorant," "awakening the negligent," and "encouraging" and "propagating" faith and righteous deeds. It is obvious that those who exhort one another to the truth must themselves be supporters of the truth and practitioners of it.

In the fourth principle, the issue of endurance, "Patience," steadfastness, and exhorting one another to it is raised. This is because, after the issue of recognition and awareness, everyone encounters obstacles at every step in the path of action. If one does not have steadfastness and patience, he can never realize the truth, perform a righteous deed, or preserve his faith. Yes, realizing the truth, implementing the truth, and fulfilling the truth in society is not possible except through a collective movement, decision-making, and steadfastness against obstacles. "Patience" here also has a broad and extensive meaning that includes patience in obedience, patience against the motives of sin, and patience in the face of calamities, unpleasant events, and the loss of strengths, capitals, and fruits. [1] Considering what was said above regarding these four principles—which are truly the most comprehensive program for the life and happiness of human beings—it becomes clear why it is mentioned in narrations that "when the companions and followers of the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله met one another, before they separated, they would recite Surah 'Al-Asr' and remind themselves of the great content of this small Surah, then they would say goodbye to each other and go about their work." [1] And truly, if Muslims today implemented these same four principles in their individual and social lives, their problems and disorders would be solved, backwardness would be compensated for, weaknesses and defeats would turn into victory, and the evil of the world's wicked ones would be cut off from them.

O Allah! Grant us patience, steadfastness, and the success of exhorting one another to truth and patience. O Lord! We are all in loss, and the compensation for this loss is not possible except through Your grace. O God! We love to be practitioners of these four instructions You have given in this Surah; make success the companion of our path.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Asr.

[1]: Regarding the reality of "Patience" and its stages and branches, we had a detailed discussion in Volume 1 under Verse 153 of Surah Al-Baqarah. [1]: "Ad-Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 392.
